authoritySource: Dr. Ahmed Hesham — OET / IELTS / PTE Grammar Rulebook for Healthcare Professionals v1

rules.title: Visits in past simple
rules.body: Describe every completed consultation in the past simple. 'The patient presented with chest pain' — never 'The patient has presented with chest pain' for a single completed visit.
exemplarPhrases: Mrs Jones presented with a three-day history of cough.
The patient reported intermittent headaches.

good: Mr Patel attended clinic three weeks ago with back pain.
bad: Mr Patel has attended clinic three weeks ago with back pain.

rules.title: Resolved surgery is past simple
rules.body: All resolved conditions and completed surgical procedures use past simple. Never present perfect for surgery.
exemplarPhrases: She underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy in 2018.
He had an appendicectomy at age 12.

bad: She has undergone a cholecystectomy in 2018.

rules.title: Ongoing facts in present simple
rules.body: Use the present simple for ongoing medical, social, and family history — conditions still true at the time of writing.
exemplarPhrases: She has hypertension.
He smokes 20 cigarettes per day.

rules.title: Smoking/drinking with duration = present perfect continuous
rules.body: Smoking and drinking with a duration use present perfect continuous; without duration they use present simple.
exemplarPhrases: She has been smoking for 20 years.
He drinks alcohol socially.

rules.title: Medical records take present simple reveal/show
rules.body: Use present simple after 'Mr X's medical records' — 'reveal', 'show', 'indicate'.
exemplarPhrases: Mr Jones' medical records reveal a history of asthma.

rules.title: Over the last X first sentence = present perfect
rules.body: When a paragraph opens with 'Over the last [X]', the first sentence uses present perfect; remaining sentences may drop to past simple.

rules.title: Since + starting point = present perfect
rules.body: Sentences with 'since' + a starting point require present perfect. Never past simple.
exemplarPhrases: She has had hypertension since 2010.
He has been on insulin since last year.

rules.title: For + duration = present perfect
rules.body: Sentences with 'for' + a duration require present perfect.
exemplarPhrases: She has had hypertension for five years.
He has been taking metformin for three months.

bad: She has hypertension for five years.
good: She has had hypertension for five years.

rules.title: X ago = past simple
rules.body: '[X] ago' always requires past simple. Present perfect with 'ago' is ungrammatical.
exemplarPhrases: He presented three weeks ago.
She was diagnosed two years ago.

bad: She has presented three weeks ago.

rules.title: Present perfect for symptoms still present
rules.body: Use present perfect only when the symptom is still present at the time of writing. Never use present perfect for vital-sign values.
exemplarPhrases: She has been experiencing fatigue for two weeks.

rules.title: Avoid past perfect
rules.body: Past perfect (had + past participle) is poorly rewarded in clinical letters. Prefer the -ING form or a noun phrase.
exemplarPhrases: She reported having headaches.
She described a history of headaches.

rules.title: Clinical admission = passive
rules.body: Admissions use the passive voice with 'admitted to ... with'. Never 'was presented' as passive of 'presented'.
exemplarPhrases: Mr Jones was admitted to St Mary's Hospital with chest pain.

rules.title: Prescribed/advised in passive
rules.body: Treatment verbs 'prescribe', 'advise', 'refer', 'review' read naturally in the passive when the agent is clinical staff.
exemplarPhrases: She was prescribed amoxicillin 500 mg three times a day.
He was advised on lifestyle modification.

rules.title: Passive keeps focus on patient/result
rules.body: Use passive to keep focus on the patient or the investigation result, not the staff member who performed it.
exemplarPhrases: An ECG was performed, which revealed ST elevation.

rules.title: Passive not mandatory in every sentence
rules.body: Do not force every sentence into passive. Mix passive and active to retain clarity.

rules.title: Diseases take no article
rules.body: Named conditions take no article: 'She has hypertension', 'He developed pneumonia'. Do not write 'a hypertension' or 'the pneumonia'.
exemplarPhrases: She has asthma.
He was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus.

rules.title: Symptom + article article rules
rules.body: Countable symptoms take 'a': 'a headache', 'a cough'. Uncountable symptoms take no article: 'nausea', 'fatigue', 'dizziness'.
exemplarPhrases: She complained of a headache and nausea.
He reported fatigue and dizziness.

rules.title: Body parts take possessive pronoun
rules.body: Body parts generally take a possessive pronoun rather than 'the': 'her left knee', not 'the left knee', when belonging to the patient.
exemplarPhrases: The examination of her left knee revealed effusion.

rules.title: Investigations with an initial vowel sound take 'an'
rules.body: Use 'an' before abbreviations whose first spoken letter is a vowel sound: 'an ECG', 'an MRI', 'an X-ray', 'an ENT referral'.
exemplarPhrases: An ECG was performed.
An MRI of the brain was arranged.

rules.title: Level takes article; count takes none
rules.body: 'Glucose level' can take an article ('an increased glucose level'). Plural counts take no article ('increased white cell counts').

rules.title: Agreement with closest subject after 'or'
rules.body: When two subjects are connected by 'or', the verb agrees with the CLOSER subject. 'Neither Mr Jones nor his parents are aware' (parents → plural).
exemplarPhrases: Neither the GP nor the parents are aware of the results.

rules.title: Reverse rule with 'along with' / 'together with' / 'in addition to'
rules.body: 'Along with', 'together with', 'as well as' and 'in addition to' do NOT change the number of the subject. The verb agrees with the word BEFORE the linker.
exemplarPhrases: Mr Jones, along with his wife, was informed of the diagnosis.

rules.title: Collective nouns take singular
rules.body: 'Team', 'staff', 'family', 'committee' take a singular verb in OET letters.
exemplarPhrases: The team was consulted regarding further management.

rules.title: Medical plurals agree plural
rules.body: 'Symptoms', 'investigations', 'medications' are plural and take plural verbs.
exemplarPhrases: Her symptoms have persisted despite treatment.

rules.title: Zero conditional for clinical facts
rules.body: Zero conditional (if + present simple, present simple) describes predictable medical facts.
exemplarPhrases: If blood pressure rises above 140/90, antihypertensive therapy is indicated.

rules.title: First conditional for likely outcomes
rules.body: First conditional (if + present simple, will + base) describes likely outcomes of the current plan.
exemplarPhrases: If her symptoms persist, we will arrange further imaging.

rules.title: Second conditional for hypotheticals
rules.body: Second conditional (if + past simple, would + base) describes hypothetical management.
exemplarPhrases: If she were to experience further chest pain, she would need to attend the emergency department.

rules.title: Third conditional for retrospective comment
rules.body: Third conditional describes past hypothetical outcomes; use sparingly in clinical letters.

rules.title: No contractions in clinical letters
rules.body: Never use contractions in OET, IELTS General (formal) or PTE writing. Write 'do not', 'cannot', 'I am', 'have not'.

rules.title: Never use 'the patient' in body
rules.body: Never refer to the patient as 'the patient' in the body of a letter. Use title + last name for adults, first name for children, or a pronoun.

rules.title: No 'smoker' / 'drinker' labels
rules.body: Never label patients 'smoker' or 'drinker'. Write 'she smokes' or 'he drinks alcohol socially'.

rules.title: Conditions lowercase (except eponymous)
rules.body: Medical conditions are lowercase: hypertension, asthma, type 2 diabetes. Eponymous diseases keep the capital: Parkinson's, Crohn's, Alzheimer's.

good: Mr Jones has hypertension and type 2 diabetes.
bad: Mr Jones has Hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes.

rules.title: Replace colloquial with clinical
rules.body: Replace colloquial terms: 'tired' → 'fatigued'; 'bad' → 'poor'/'inadequate'; 'sick' → 'unwell'.

rules.title: No patient labels (anxious, non-compliant)
rules.body: Never label patients 'anxious', 'non-compliant'. Describe behaviour: 'she expressed concern', 'he reported difficulty adhering'.

rules.title: Use 'gentleman' / 'lady' in appositives
rules.body: In appositive clauses, use 'gentleman' and 'lady' rather than 'man' and 'woman'.
exemplarPhrases: Mrs Miller, a 65-year-old lady, presented with chest pain.

rules.title: However = semicolon before, comma after
rules.body: The adverb 'however' joining two independent clauses takes a semicolon before and a comma after. Pattern: '…; however, …'.
exemplarPhrases: She completed the course; however, her symptoms persisted.

rules.title: Therefore / Thus = semicolon before, comma after
rules.body: 'Therefore' and 'thus' joining clauses follow the same pattern as 'however': '…; therefore, …'.
exemplarPhrases: Her HbA1c rose to 9.2%; therefore, insulin was initiated.

rules.title: In addition = semicolon before, comma after
rules.body: 'In addition' joining two independent clauses takes a semicolon before and a comma after.
exemplarPhrases: She was advised on lifestyle modification; in addition, dietetic review was arranged.

rules.title: In addition to = no preceding punctuation
rules.body: 'In addition to', 'along with', 'as well as', 'together with' are NOT adverbials. No preceding comma/semicolon; followed by a noun phrase or -ING form.
exemplarPhrases: In addition to lifestyle advice, metformin was commenced.

rules.title: As (meaning because) takes a comma
rules.body: When 'as' means 'because' in mid-sentence, a comma precedes it.
exemplarPhrases: She was referred urgently, as her symptoms had progressed.

rules.title: Max 1–2 linkers per paragraph
rules.body: Do not use more than 1–2 inter-sentence linkers per paragraph. Excessive linkers reduce the Conciseness and Clarity score.

rules.title: Sentence length guide 15–25 words
rules.body: Target 15–25 words per sentence. Do not chain three or more clauses. Avoid comma splices.

rules.title: Treatment FOR not FROM
rules.body: Use 'treatment for', 'admitted for', 'referred for', 'managed for'. Never 'treatment from' / 'admitted from'.

rules.title: On + date; in + month/year; at + time
rules.body: Use 'on' with dates, 'in' with months or years, 'at' with clock times.
exemplarPhrases: She was admitted on 15 March.
The procedure was performed in 2018.
The appointment is at 10:30 a.m.

rules.title: Preferred 'three months ago' over dates
rules.body: Prefer elapsed-time phrases ('three months ago', 'two weeks prior') over exact dates in body paragraphs.

rules.title: Duration adjective hyphenated and singular
rules.body: Duration used as adjective before a noun: hyphenate and keep singular. 'A six-month history of acne' — not 'a six months history'.
exemplarPhrases: A three-week history of cough.

rules.title: Despite + noun / -ING
rules.body: 'Despite' is followed by a noun or an -ING form, NOT a full clause.
exemplarPhrases: Despite the medication, her symptoms persisted.
Despite taking the antibiotics, she continued to deteriorate.

rules.title: Although + full clause
rules.body: 'Although' is followed by a full clause (subject + verb).
exemplarPhrases: Although the medication was prescribed, her symptoms persisted.

rules.title: Whereas contrasts parallel ideas
rules.body: 'Whereas' contrasts two parallel ideas; use sparingly.

rules.title: Reported speech uses -ING or noun phrase
rules.body: After 'reported', 'mentioned', 'stated', 'described', use -ING or a noun phrase rather than a nested past perfect clause.
exemplarPhrases: She reported having headaches.
He described a history of chest pain.

rules.title: Avoid 'had had'
rules.body: 'Had had' is grammatical but poorly rewarded in clinical letters. Prefer -ING or a noun phrase.

rules.title: Advised to / counselled regarding
rules.body: Use 'advised to adopt', 'counselled regarding' — never 'instructed to adopt'.
exemplarPhrases: She was advised to adopt lifestyle modifications.
He was counselled regarding alcohol intake.

rules.title: Latin abbreviations MUST be translated
rules.body: Translate Latin dose abbreviations: od/om → once a day; bd/bid → twice a day; tds/tid → three times a day; qds/qid → four times a day; stat → immediately; prn → as needed.

rules.title: Always pair values with units
rules.body: Clinical values always include their unit: 'glucose of 7.8 mmol/L', 'BP 150/90 mmHg', '38.5 °C'. Never write the reference range in the letter.

rules.title: Numbers 1–9 words; 10+ numerals
rules.body: Numbers 1–9 in running text are spelled out; 10 and above are numerals. Always use numerals with units.

rules.title: Never abbreviate the year
rules.body: Never abbreviate the year (write 2026, not '26).

rules.title: Single-digit dates take leading zero
rules.body: Dates 1–9 always carry a leading zero: 01, 02, …, 09.

rules.title: Oral in full; IM/IV/SC/SL abbreviations acceptable
rules.body: Write 'oral' in full (never 'PO'); IM, IV, SC, SL abbreviations are acceptable.
